validation.validation_summary:
The baseline incident view is structurally incomplete and analytically premature. The correlation report is entirely placeholder with no synthesized hypotheses, correlations, or timeline. Of 32 chunks, only 7 contain real summaries (chunks 1, 11, 14, 15, 18, 20, and partially chunk 5 via category counts); the remaining 25 are unanalyzed. This means the current view reflects at most ~22% of the available evidence.

The most critical unaddressed anomaly is the system_configuration event spike in chunks 28–29 (8,704 and 8,586 events respectively), which is 700–870x above baseline for that category across all other chunks. This could represent a mass configuration change, a miscategorized log flood, or a rootkit/persistence mechanism — it has received zero analyst attention.

Among the analyzed chunks, the strongest confirmed findings are: (1) a sustained, multi-source SSH brute-force campaign across June–December 2015 (chunks 11, 14, 15, 18, 20) — this is well-evidenced and likely represents internet background scanning rather than a targeted coordinated attack; (2) a confirmed successful SSH login by user 'fztu' from 119.137.62.142 during an active brute-force window (chunk 20) — this is the highest-priority finding and requires immediate follow-up on session activity; (3) chronic mod_jk connector errors in 2005 (chunk 1) — likely a known connector bug rather than a service outage, but unconfirmed without Tomcat-side logs.

Key overconfidence risks: the 'test' account sessions are rated HIGH without evidence of malicious activity; the Kerberos replay events have not been ruled out as NTP-skew artifacts; the single directory-listing probe is rated MEDIUM without distinguishing it from crawler noise; and the three date ranges (2005, mid-2015, late-2015) may represent different hosts, making cross-chunk correlation unreliable without host-identity confirmation. All line_range values being [1,1] suggests a metadata parsing bug that should be investigated before trusting event ordering or chunk boundaries.

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: The mod_jk persistent error states represent a systemic, unresolved connector failure causing service degradation.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The mod_jk 'child workerEnv in error state' errors are rated HIGH severity and described as 'systemic mod_jk connector failure.' However, the evidence is limited to a single chunk covering ~1.5 hours in 2005. No HTTP error rates, Tomcat logs, or application-level impact evidence is present.
counterevidence: mod_jk2 (the JK2 connector) was deprecated and known to have logging bugs where error states 6–7 were emitted spuriously during normal child recycling.
The Apache restart at 05:04 UTC could explain the burst of errors as a normal post-restart initialization sequence rather than a chronic failure.
No evidence of HTTP 502/503 responses, application downtime, or user-facing impact is present in the provided data.
The errors persist for ~1.5 hours without any escalation or intervention, which is unusual if the service were truly non-functional.
evidence_gaps: Apache access logs for the same time window to check for HTTP 5xx error rates.
Tomcat catalina.out logs to determine if the backend was actually rejecting connections.
mod_jk2 version to cross-reference against known logging bugs.
System uptime and load metrics around 05:04 UTC to confirm whether a crash-recovery or scheduled restart occurred.

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: The single 'Directory index forbidden' event from 222.166.160.184 represents a deliberate directory-listing reconnaissance probe.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The directory-listing probe from 222.166.160.184 is rated MEDIUM severity as 'reconnaissance.' A single 403-equivalent event from one IP over a ~1.5-hour window is very weak evidence for deliberate reconnaissance.
counterevidence: Only one event from this IP is recorded in the entire dataset — a single access attempt does not constitute a scanning pattern.
'Directory index forbidden' errors are routinely generated by search-engine crawlers, misconfigured CDN health checks, and browser prefetch requests.
No follow-on probes, path traversal attempts, or vulnerability scans from this IP are documented in any chunk.
The IP appears only once across all 32 chunks.
evidence_gaps: Apache access log for the full request from 222.166.160.184 including User-Agent string, referrer, and any subsequent requests.
Reverse DNS and WHOIS lookup for 222.166.160.184 to determine if it is a known crawler or scanner.
Check whether 222.166.160.184 appears in any other chunk's logs.
Frequency of 'Directory index forbidden' events from other IPs in the same time window to establish a baseline.

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: The 'test' account (uid=509) SSH sessions represent unauthorized post-compromise access or scripted credential validation.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The 'test' account (uid=509) SSH sessions in chunks 11 and 14 are flagged as HIGH severity and described as 'post-compromise probing or credential validation.' This is speculative. The sessions open and close cleanly, which is atypical for post-compromise activity where an attacker would maintain persistence.
counterevidence: Sessions close immediately and cleanly — an attacker who successfully authenticated would typically maintain the session or execute commands.
A 'test' account with uid=509 is a non-privileged account; post-compromise probing typically targets higher-privilege accounts.
No evidence of privilege escalation, lateral movement, or unusual process execution following the test account sessions is present in any chunk.
The account may be a legitimate low-privilege service or monitoring account.
evidence_gaps: /var/log/secure or auth.log entries showing what commands (if any) were run during the test account sessions.
/etc/passwd entry for uid=509 to confirm account purpose and shell.
Check whether the source IP of the test account logins matches any known internal or monitoring IP range.
Audit logs (auditd) for uid=509 activity during and after the session windows.

alternative_explanations.rationale: The SSH and FTP probes span months (June–December 2015) from geographically diverse IPs with no apparent coordination. This pattern is entirely consistent with automated internet-wide scanners (Shodan, Masscan, Mirai-era bots) that hit every reachable port 22/21 indiscriminately. No evidence of shared C2, shared wordlists, or synchronized timing across the different source IPs is present in the provided data.

alternative_explanations.explanation: Log rotation artifact, repeated syslog message, or miscategorized bulk output
competes_with: Chunks 28–29 system_configuration spike (8704/8586 events) = mass config change or attack
alternative_explanations.rationale: Chunks 28 and 29 show system_configuration counts two orders of magnitude above all other chunks (8704 and 8586 vs. ~10 elsewhere) yet have placeholder summaries and no suspicious events. This is more likely a categorization error, a repeated syslog line being counted individually, or a bulk config-file read being logged verbosely than a genuine mass reconfiguration event. The absence of any analyst annotation is a strong signal of unreviewed data.

alternative_explanations.explanation: Known mod_jk 1.x / JK2 bug where error states 6–7 are logged spuriously on graceful child recycling
competes_with: mod_jk persistent error state = systemic connector failure
alternative_explanations.rationale: mod_jk2 (the JK2 connector, distinct from mod_jk) had documented bugs where workerEnv error states were logged even when the backend Tomcat was healthy. The fact that the errors fire immediately after every init success—rather than after actual request failures—is more consistent with a logging artifact or a known connector quirk than a true backend outage. No evidence of HTTP 502/503 responses or Tomcat-side errors is present in the provided chunks.

overconfidence_signals: Over 75% of chunks (25 of 32) have placeholder or test summaries — the correlation report's global_summary is also a placeholder. Any global hypothesis drawn from this dataset is based on a small, potentially unrepresentative sample.

The correlation report has empty key_correlations, hypotheses, timeline_highlights, and next_queries arrays, yet the chunk summaries assert HIGH and CRITICAL severity findings. There is a structural disconnect between the evidence layer and the synthesis layer.

Chunks 28 and 29 have system_configuration counts of 8704 and 8586 respectively — orders of magnitude above all other chunks — yet are completely unanalyzed. Ignoring these is a major overconfidence signal; the most anomalous data points have received zero scrutiny.

Chunk 17 contains 195 authz_security events with no summary and no suspicious events flagged. A chunk with that volume of security events being silently passed over inflates confidence in the completeness of the threat picture.

The 'test' account sessions are rated HIGH severity based solely on the open/close pattern without any evidence of malicious commands, privilege escalation, or post-session anomalies — severity is overstated relative to available evidence.
suggested_queries: SELECT timestamp, source_ip, username, result FROM ssh_auth_log WHERE source_ip IN ('103.207.39.212','103.207.39.16') ORDER BY timestamp — confirm whether the /24 subnet represents a single actor or shared infrastructure across chunks 18 and 20.
grep -E 'Accepted|opened' /var/log/secure | grep 'test\|fztu' — retrieve all successful logins for the 'test' (uid=509) and 'fztu' accounts to determine if these are routine or anomalous.

grep '222.166.160.184' /var/log/httpd/access_log — retrieve the full HTTP request record including User-Agent, referrer, and any subsequent requests from this IP to distinguish crawler from targeted probe.
SELECT * FROM syslog WHERE chunk_id IN (28,29) LIMIT 100 — sample the raw events driving the 8704/8586 system_configuration counts to identify the message type and source process.
SELECT DISTINCT message_type, COUNT(*) FROM syslog WHERE chunk_id IN (28,29) GROUP BY message_type ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC — identify the dominant log message causing the spike.
grep -E 'error_state|workerEnv' /var/log/httpd/error_log | grep -v 'init\|jk2_init' — isolate mod_jk error-state events excluding init messages to measure true error frequency vs. init frequency.
cat /var/log/tomcat/catalina.out | grep -E '2005-12-04 0[4-6]' — check Tomcat backend logs for the same 04:47–06:19 UTC window to determine if the mod_jk errors correspond to actual backend failures.
ntpq -p (or equivalent historical NTP drift logs) for the host running Kerberos around 2015-06-30 20:53 UTC — rule out clock skew as the cause of replay-cache errors from 163.27.187.39.

chunk_overview.summary: This chunk covers Apache/mod_jk activity on 2005-12-04 from ~04:47 to ~06:19 UTC. Three recurring event types dominate: (E1) jk2_init child discovery notices, (E2) workerEnv.init() success notices, and (E3) mod_jk child workerEnv error-state errors. The E3 errors fire persistently and immediately after E2 init successes throughout the entire window, indicating a chronic mod_jk connector instability — workers initialize but immediately enter error states 6 or 7. A notable burst at 05:04:03–05:04:04 shows 7 children discovered simultaneously followed by 7 paired init/error cycles, suggesting a full Apache restart. One distinct security event (E4) at 05:15:09 records an external IP (222.166.160.184) triggering a "Directory index forbidden" error on /var/www/html/, indicating a directory-listing probe attempt.

Chunk 11 spans 2015-06-29 to 2015-07-03 and is dominated by three threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against user root from 60.30.224.116 and 195.129.24.210, and against unknown users from biblioteka.wsi.edu.pl and zummit.com; (2) a burst of Kerberos authentication failures from 163.27.187.39 with "replay cache" and "software caused connection abort" errors suggesting a replay/credential-stuffing attack; (3) anomalous mass SSH sessions opened and immediately closed for the generic "test" account (uid=509) across multiple timestamps, consistent with scripted access or scanning. FTP connection bursts from 202.82.200.188, 203.101.45.59, and 62.99.164.82 (20+ connections each in under one second) also indicate automated FTP probing. Recurring logrotate ALERT exits and a syslogd restart on 2015-07-03 are notable operational anomalies.

This chunk spans 2015-07-11 to 2015-07-17 and is dominated by two major threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against the root account from at least four distinct external IPs (211.137.205.253, 68.143.156.89, 202-132-40-29.adsl.ttn.net, c51471f2c.cable.wanadoo.nl, 61-220-159-99.hinet-ip.hinet.net), and (2) a massive FTP connection flood from multiple IPs (211.107.232.1, 212.65.68.82, 83.116.207.11, 218.146.61.230, 210.245.165.136, 207.30.238.8, 203.101.45.59) with dozens of connections per source within seconds, strongly indicative of automated scanning or brute-force tooling. Recurring daily logrotate ALERT failures (logrotate exited abnormally with [1]) suggest a persistent misconfiguration. A brief SSH session for the generic 'test' account (uid=509) on 2015-07-13 is anomalous. Routine scheduled su sessions for 'cyrus' and 'news' by root (uid=0) appear daily around 04:00 UTC, consistent with cron-driven maintenance. A cupsd restart and syslogd restart occurred on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window.

This chunk (2015-07-17 to 2015-07-24) is dominated by two threat patterns: (1) high-volume burst FTP connection floods from at least 8 distinct external IPs — several with no reverse-DNS — strongly suggesting automated scanning or brute-force tooling against ftpd; one session from 84.102.20.2 escalated to an actual anonymous FTP login. (2) Repeated SSH authentication failures against unknown users and root from multiple Asian/European IPs (211-76-104-65.ebix.net.tw, 202.181.236.180, 218.55.234.102, 210.76.59.29, 193.110.106.11, 85.44.47.166, 211.9.58.217), consistent with distributed SSH brute-force campaigns. Routine scheduled activity (daily logrotate ALERT with exit code 1, su sessions for cyrus/news by uid=0) recurs every morning around 04:00 UTC across all days in the chunk.

This chunk is dominated by sustained SSH brute-force credential-stuffing attacks originating from at least six distinct external IPs (5.188.10.180, 103.207.39.212, 106.5.5.195, 52.80.34.196, 185.190.58.151, 103.99.0.122) across a ~46-minute window (08:26–09:12 UTC on 2015-12-10). Attackers systematically cycled through common usernames (default, ftp, guest, support, admin, root, uucp, matlab, 0, 123, 1234, anonymous, ubnt, cisco, test, user, operator, monitor, ftpuser, pi, PlcmSpIp, sshd). Multiple PAM retry-limit violations were recorded, with PAM explicitly ignoring max retries (5 > 3, 6 > 3). All events originate from openssh/sshd and fall squarely in the authz_security category. No successful logins were observed. Several probes dropped the connection before completing authentication (preauth disconnects), consistent with automated scanning tools.

This chunk is dominated by a sustained, multi-source SSH brute-force campaign spanning roughly 09:17–10:14 UTC on 2015-12-10. The primary attacker is 187.141.143.180 (customer-187-141-143-180-sta.uninet-ide.com.mx), which cycles through dozens of common/service usernames (postgres, nagios, www, abc, ted, vnc, git, ghost, ubuntu, ftp, test, deploy, oralce, oracle, nagios1, postgres1, test1, test2, bssh, mysql, magnos, ingrid, jay, cyrus) in rapid succession (~5-second intervals), generating repeated "Invalid user", "Failed password", PAM auth failures, and "POSSIBLE BREAK-IN ATTEMPT" (reverse DNS mismatch) events. A second attacker, 103.207.39.16, joins concurrently targeting support, uucp, admin, and deploy. A third source, 104.192.3.34, attempts FILTER and root. A fourth, 60.2.12.12, hammers root 5 times in ~30 seconds. A fifth, 119.4.203.64, repeatedly fails on admin from the same port (2191) 6 times in 14 seconds. A sixth, 52.80.34.196 (AWS China), tries matlab. Notably, one successful login is recorded: user fztu from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20Z, with a session lasting ~13 minutes before clean disconnect at 09:45:06Z. A "Did not receive identification string" probe is also observed from 103.207.39.16 and 181.214.87.4.

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: The SSH brute-force activity in chunks 18 and 20 represents a coordinated multi-source attack campaign against this host.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The 'coordinated multi-source attack' framing assumes the concurrent SSH brute-force IPs in chunks 18 and 20 are part of a single campaign. However, the IPs span multiple ASNs and geographies (Mexico, China/AWS, unknown), and the username lists partially overlap but are not identical. Coordination is asserted but not demonstrated.
counterevidence: 5.188.10.180 (chunk 18) and 187.141.143.180 (chunk 20) use different username sequences and are separated by ~51 minutes — consistent with independent bots, not a coordinated campaign.
103.207.39.212 (chunk 18) and 103.207.39.16 (chunk 20) share a /24 subnet, which could indicate a single actor, but could also be a shared hosting provider or VPN exit node.
No shared session tokens, identical payloads, or synchronized timing windows are documented.
evidence_gaps: ASN/WHOIS lookups for all attacking IPs to determine if they share infrastructure.
Comparison of exact username lists across all attacking IPs to identify shared wordlists.
Check whether 103.207.39.x IPs appear in threat-intel feeds as known scanner infrastructure.
Packet captures or full sshd verbose logs to compare SSH client version strings across IPs.

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: The baseline incident view provides a reliable, high-confidence picture of the incident across all log data.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The correlation report is entirely placeholder ('global_summary: placeholder', empty key_correlations, hypotheses, timeline_highlights, next_queries). No actual cross-chunk correlation has been performed. All findings derive from only 7 of 32 chunks having real summaries; the remaining 25 are 'placeholder' or 'test'. Confidence in any global hypothesis is therefore very low.
counterevidence: Chunks 2–10, 12–13, 16–17, 19, 21–24, 26–32 all have placeholder summaries — over 75% of the dataset is unanalyzed.
Chunks 28 and 29 have anomalously high system_configuration counts (8704, 8586) with no explanation, suggesting a potential major event that has been entirely missed.
Chunk 17 has 195 authz_security events with no summary or suspicious events flagged — a large blind spot.
Chunk 8 has zero events across all categories, which may indicate a log gap or parsing failure rather than a quiet period.
evidence_gaps: Full summaries and suspicious-event extraction for chunks 2–10, 12–13, 16–17, 19, 21–24, 26–32.
Explanation for the system_configuration spike in chunks 28–29.
Clarification of whether chunk 8's zero counts represent a true quiet period or a data ingestion failure.
Timeline continuity check: are the three date ranges (2005-12-04, 2015-06-29–07-24, 2015-12-10) from the same host or different hosts/log files?

challenged_hypotheses.hypothesis: Chunks 28 and 29 contain only routine system_configuration noise comparable to other chunks.
challenged_hypotheses.challenge: The system_configuration event spikes in chunks 28 (8704) and 29 (8586) are completely unanalyzed. These counts are 700–870x higher than any other chunk's system_configuration counts (~8–12). This could represent a critical event (mass config change, rootkit activity, kernel parameter manipulation) or a data quality issue (miscategorization, log verbosity burst). The absence of any analysis makes any hypothesis about these chunks unreliable.
counterevidence: All other chunks with system_configuration events show counts of 5–12, making 8704/8586 statistically extreme outliers.
Both chunks have placeholder summaries and zero suspicious events flagged — inconsistent with genuine high-volume security events which would typically trigger automated alerts.
Chunks 25–27 and 30–32 surrounding these spikes show normal system_configuration counts (8–12), suggesting the spike is isolated.
evidence_gaps: Full summary and event-level analysis of chunks 28 and 29.
Identification of the specific syslog source and message type generating the bulk of system_configuration events in these chunks.
Timestamp range for chunks 28 and 29 to determine if they overlap with any known maintenance window.
Check whether the spike correlates with a kernel upgrade, configuration management tool run (Puppet/Chef/Ansible), or syslog misconfiguration.

No evidence of HTTP 502/503 responses or Tomcat-side errors is present in the provided chunks.

alternative_explanations.explanation: Misconfigured or clock-skewed Kerberos client causing legitimate replay-cache collisions
alternative_explanations.rationale: Kerberos replay-cache errors are frequently caused by NTP skew (>5 min) or a misconfigured client retransmitting tickets. The 'software caused connection abort' storm immediately following could be the client-side TCP stack reacting to server-side resets after the replay rejection, not an attacker tool. No evidence of credential theft or follow-on access from 163.27.187.39 is present.

alternative_explanations.explanation: Legitimate user login coinciding with background brute-force noise
competes_with: fztu login from 119.137.62.142 = successful brute-force compromise
alternative_explanations.rationale: The fztu session (09:32–09:45 UTC, ~13 min, clean disconnect) could be a legitimate user whose credentials were never at risk from the concurrent brute-force. The brute-force IPs (187.141.143.180, 103.207.39.16, etc.) were targeting service/invalid accounts, not 'fztu'. Without evidence that 119.137.62.142 is anomalous for this user, or that commands run during the session were malicious, this login cannot be attributed to a successful attack.

alternative_explanations.explanation: Legitimate developer/ops account used for automated health checks or CI/CD pipeline
alternative_explanations.rationale: A uid=509 'test' account with rapid open/close SSH sessions could be a monitoring script, a CI runner, or a deployment tool. The sessions close cleanly rather than being forcibly terminated. Without evidence of unusual commands, lateral movement, or privilege escalation following the sessions, post-compromise probing is not confirmed.

overconfidence_signals: Over 75% of chunks (25 of 32) have placeholder or test summaries — the correlation report's global_summary is also a placeholder. Any global hypothesis drawn from this dataset is based on a small, potentially unrepresentative sample.

The correlation report has empty key_correlations, hypotheses, timeline_highlights, and next_queries arrays, yet the chunk summaries assert HIGH and CRITICAL severity findings. There is a structural disconnect between the evidence layer and the synthesis layer.

Chunks 28 and 29 have system_configuration counts of 8704 and 8586 respectively — orders of magnitude above all other chunks — yet are completely unanalyzed. Ignoring these is a major overconfidence signal; the most anomalous data points have received zero scrutiny.

Chunk 17 contains 195 authz_security events with no summary and no suspicious events flagged. A chunk with that volume of security events being silently passed over inflates confidence in the completeness of the threat picture.

The 'test' account sessions are rated HIGH severity based solely on the open/close pattern without any evidence of malicious commands, privilege escalation, or post-session anomalies — severity is overstated relative to available evidence.
The Kerberos replay attack hypothesis (chunk 11) is rated HIGH without ruling out NTP skew or client misconfiguration, which are far more common causes of replay-cache errors.

The fztu successful login (chunk 20) is noted but not explicitly rated or correlated with the concurrent brute-force — the absence of a severity rating for a confirmed successful login during an active attack window is an analytical gap that could lead to underestimating compromise risk.

The three date ranges in the dataset (2005-12-04, 2015-06-29 to 2015-07-24, 2015-12-10) span a decade and may represent entirely different hosts or log files. Treating them as a single incident timeline without confirming they share a common host is an overconfidence signal.
All line_range values are [1,1] across all chunks, suggesting a metadata parsing issue that may have affected event ordering, deduplication, or chunk boundary accuracy.
suggested_queries: SELECT timestamp, source_ip, username, result FROM ssh_auth_log WHERE source_ip IN ('103.207.39.212','103.207.39.16') ORDER BY timestamp — confirm whether the /24 subnet represents a single actor or shared infrastructure across chunks 18 and 20.
grep -E 'Accepted|opened' /var/log/secure | grep 'test\|fztu' — retrieve all successful logins for the 'test' (uid=509) and 'fztu' accounts to determine if these are routine or anomalous.

grep '222.166.160.184' /var/log/httpd/access_log — retrieve the full HTTP request record including User-Agent, referrer, and any subsequent requests from this IP to distinguish crawler from targeted probe.
SELECT * FROM syslog WHERE chunk_id IN (28,29) LIMIT 100 — sample the raw events driving the 8704/8586 system_configuration counts to identify the message type and source process.
SELECT DISTINCT message_type, COUNT(*) FROM syslog WHERE chunk_id IN (28,29) GROUP BY message_type ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC — identify the dominant log message causing the spike.
grep -E 'error_state|workerEnv' /var/log/httpd/error_log | grep -v 'init\|jk2_init' — isolate mod_jk error-state events excluding init messages to measure true error frequency vs. init frequency.
cat /var/log/tomcat/catalina.out | grep -E '2005-12-04 0[4-6]' — check Tomcat backend logs for the same 04:47–06:19 UTC window to determine if the mod_jk errors correspond to actual backend failures.
ntpq -p (or equivalent historical NTP drift logs) for the host running Kerberos around 2015-06-30 20:53 UTC — rule out clock skew as the cause of replay-cache errors from 163.27.187.39.

grep 'uid=509\|test' /var/log/audit/audit.log | grep -E '2015-06-30 22:1[6-8]|2015-07-13 17:2[2-4]' — retrieve auditd records for the 'test' account sessions to determine what commands, if any, were executed.

SELECT source_ip, COUNT(*) as attempts, MIN(timestamp) as first_seen, MAX(timestamp) as last_seen FROM ssh_auth_log WHERE timestamp BETWEEN '2015-12-10 08:00:00' AND '2015-12-10 10:15:00' GROUP BY source_ip ORDER BY attempts DESC — build a complete attacker IP frequency table for the December 2015 brute-force window to identify any IPs present in chunks 18/20 that also appear in the unanalyzed chunks 21–24.

grep -E 'Accepted password|Accepted publickey' /var/log/secure | grep -v 'root\|cyrus\|news' — identify all non-service-account successful logins across the full dataset to find any additional compromised sessions beyond fztu.

diff <(host 187.141.143.180) <(dig -x 187.141.143.180 +short) — verify the forward/reverse DNS mismatch for the primary December 2015 attacker to confirm the POSSIBLE BREAK-IN ATTEMPT warnings are genuine PTR mismatches and not a DNS resolution failure.

SELECT chunk_id, line_range FROM chunk_metadata WHERE line_range = '[1,1]' — investigate why all chunks report line_range [1,1]; this likely indicates a metadata extraction bug that may have caused incorrect event attribution or deduplication failures.

evidence: E3 mod_jk child workerEnv in error state 6/7 fires ~65 times across 04:47–06:19Z
Error states appear immediately after every E2 workerEnv.init() ok event
Both error states 6 and 7 observed, e.g. at 04:52:15Z states 6 and 7 appear together
At 05:04:03Z: 7 children (PIDs 8738–8766) discovered simultaneously in slots 6–12 — full restart signature
Large PID gap between 6768 (04:54:20Z) and 8527 (04:56:52Z) suggests process churn or restart
[client 222.166.160.184] Directory index forbidden by rule: /var/www/html/ at 05:15:09Z
Single external IP probing the web root for directory listing

suspicious_events.rationale: External client 222.166.160.184 attempted to access /var/www/html/ and received a 'Directory index forbidden by rule' error at 2005-12-04T05:15:09Z — consistent with a directory-listing reconnaissance probe.

Persistent and recurring mod_jk 'child workerEnv in error state' errors (states 6 and 7) fire immediately after every workerEnv.init() success throughout the entire ~1.5-hour window, indicating a systemic mod_jk connector failure that is never resolved.

At 05:04:03Z, 7 Apache child processes (PIDs 8738–8766) are discovered simultaneously across slots 6–12, followed immediately by 7 paired workerEnv init/error cycles at 05:04:04Z — strongly indicative of a full Apache server restart or crash-recovery event.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk covers Apache/mod_jk activity on 2005-12-04 from ~04:47 to ~06:19 UTC. Three recurring event types dominate: (E1) jk2_init child discovery notices, (E2) workerEnv.init() success notices, and (E3) mod_jk child workerEnv error-state errors. The E3 errors fire persistently and immediately after E2 init successes throughout the entire window, indicating a chronic mod_jk connector instability — workers initialize but immediately enter error states 6 or 7. A notable burst at 05:04:03–05:04:04 shows 7 children discovered simultaneously followed by 7 paired init/error cycles, suggesting a full Apache restart. One distinct security event (E4) at 05:15:09 records an external IP (222.166.160.184) triggering a "Directory index forbidden" error on /var/www/html/, indicating a directory-listing probe attempt.

chunk_analyses.summary: Chunk 11 spans 2015-06-29 to 2015-07-03 and is dominated by three threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against user root from 60.30.224.116 and 195.129.24.210, and against unknown users from biblioteka.wsi.edu.pl and zummit.com; (2) a burst of Kerberos authentication failures from 163.27.187.39 with "replay cache" and "software caused connection abort" errors suggesting a replay/credential-stuffing attack; (3) anomalous mass SSH sessions opened and immediately closed for the generic "test" account (uid=509) across multiple timestamps, consistent with scripted access or scanning. FTP connection bursts from 202.82.200.188, 203.101.45.59, and 62.99.164.82 (20+ connections each in under one second) also indicate automated FTP probing. Recurring logrotate ALERT exits and a syslogd restart on 2015-07-03 are notable operational anomalies.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk spans 2015-07-11 to 2015-07-17 and is dominated by two major threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against the root account from at least four distinct external IPs (211.137.205.253, 68.143.156.89, 202-132-40-29.adsl.ttn.net, c51471f2c.cable.wanadoo.nl, 61-220-159-99.hinet-ip.hinet.net), and (2) a massive FTP connection flood from multiple IPs (211.107.232.1, 212.65.68.82, 83.116.207.11, 218.146.61.230, 210.245.165.136, 207.30.238.8, 203.101.45.59) with dozens of connections per source within seconds, strongly indicative of automated scanning or brute-force tooling. Recurring daily logrotate ALERT failures (logrotate exited abnormally with [1]) suggest a persistent misconfiguration. A brief SSH session for the generic 'test' account (uid=509) on 2015-07-13 is anomalous. Routine scheduled su sessions for 'cyrus' and 'news' by root (uid=0) appear daily around 04:00 UTC, consistent with cron-driven maintenance. A cupsd restart and syslogd restart occurred on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk (2015-07-17 to 2015-07-24) is dominated by two threat patterns: (1) high-volume burst FTP connection floods from at least 8 distinct external IPs — several with no reverse-DNS — strongly suggesting automated scanning or brute-force tooling against ftpd; one session from 84.102.20.2 escalated to an actual anonymous FTP login. (2) Repeated SSH authentication failures against unknown users and root from multiple Asian/European IPs (211-76-104-65.ebix.net.tw, 202.181.236.180, 218.55.234.102, 210.76.59.29, 193.110.106.11, 85.44.47.166, 211.9.58.217), consistent with distributed SSH brute-force campaigns. Routine scheduled activity (daily logrotate ALERT with exit code 1, su sessions for cyrus/news by uid=0) recurs every morning around 04:00 UTC across all days in the chunk.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk is dominated by sustained SSH brute-force credential-stuffing attacks originating from at least six distinct external IPs (5.188.10.180, 103.207.39.212, 106.5.5.195, 52.80.34.196, 185.190.58.151, 103.99.0.122) across a ~46-minute window (08:26–09:12 UTC on 2015-12-10). Attackers systematically cycled through common usernames (default, ftp, guest, support, admin, root, uucp, matlab, 0, 123, 1234, anonymous, ubnt, cisco, test, user, operator, monitor, ftpuser, pi, PlcmSpIp, sshd). Multiple PAM retry-limit violations were recorded, with PAM explicitly ignoring max retries (5 > 3, 6 > 3). All events originate from openssh/sshd and fall squarely in the authz_security category. No successful logins were observed. Several probes dropped the connection before completing authentication (preauth disconnects), consistent with automated scanning tools.

'Did not receive identification string from 181.214.87.4' followed by 'Failed none for invalid user 0' and 'Connection closed' — anomalous SSH probe using username '0', likely automated scanner.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk is dominated by a sustained, multi-source SSH brute-force campaign spanning roughly 09:17–10:14 UTC on 2015-12-10. The primary attacker is 187.141.143.180 (customer-187-141-143-180-sta.uninet-ide.com.mx), which cycles through dozens of common/service usernames (postgres, nagios, www, abc, ted, vnc, git, ghost, ubuntu, ftp, test, deploy, oralce, oracle, nagios1, postgres1, test1, test2, bssh, mysql, magnos, ingrid, jay, cyrus) in rapid succession (~5-second intervals), generating repeated "Invalid user", "Failed password", PAM auth failures, and "POSSIBLE BREAK-IN ATTEMPT" (reverse DNS mismatch) events. A second attacker, 103.207.39.16, joins concurrently targeting support, uucp, admin, and deploy. A third source, 104.192.3.34, attempts FILTER and root. A fourth, 60.2.12.12, hammers root 5 times in ~30 seconds. A fifth, 119.4.203.64, repeatedly fails on admin from the same port (2191) 6 times in 14 seconds. A sixth, 52.80.34.196 (AWS China), tries matlab. Notably, one successful login is recorded: user fztu from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20Z, with a session lasting ~13 minutes before clean disconnect at 09:45:06Z. A "Did not receive identification string" probe is also observed from 103.207.39.16 and 181.214.87.4.
